original_issue.title: SCOM support Linux Roadmap/confidence call with PG
original_issue.description:
The context : during the last months, customer had difficulties with integrating the latest Linux OS flavors within SCOM. There are typically two recent stories behind this : 

  

Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.5 was published Generally Available in November 2024, but wasn't supported within SCOM until a few months after – with the impact for us that we had to wait way too long before being able to offer it to our customers 
We also had a poor experience with Microsoft Support, which stated that Ubuntu 24.04 was not supported within our version of SCOM, which in fact was the case – with the impact for us that we were left with doubts on what's true or false 
The perception with the new OS integration in SCOM in our company could be summarized as follows : 

  

When a new Windows Server OS is released, the integration in SCOM of this new OS is seamless, we have literally nothing to do 
When a new Linux OS is released, the integration in SCOM is most of the time painful, it takes us hours of dealing with OpenSSL or TLS version mismatches, adjusting sudoers, deciphering cryptic error messages in the SCOM Discovery Wizard, spending time on creating Microsoft Support requests  
  

Swatch Group is considering moving Linux monitoring workflows out of SCOM.
original_issue.impact: If the solution is not provided the customer considers to move away from SCOM.

original_issue.title: [IAR] [Verohallinto] Azure Recovery Services Vault missing features
original_issue.description:
We need to understand if there plans in Recovery Services Vault PG to fix the blockers Gentax project is having when testing, designing and implementing DR strategy to their VM based application.

Background
▪ Business critical application hosted in Sweden Central Region 
▪ Azure IaaS VMs with 3 main roles: web, app and SQL DB  
▪ VM's provisioned from Market place image Windows Server 2022 
▪ VM Encryption at host with CMK 
▪ SQL server SKU FXmdsV2 series, NVMe interface, accelerated network 
▪ Premium SSD v1 OS Disk, Premium SSD v2 data disks, SQL Log on Ultra disk 
▪ RSV backup, App & Web VM all disks, SQL VM with DB data disk and DB log disks excluded 
▪ Requirement for in zone, cross zone and cross region restore for VM– Blockers / issues in VM restore fom RSV currently 
▪ Solution implemented for SQL DB backup to URL using Azure Storage Account with local and Geo DR 
▪ Requirement is to ensure and maintain consistency throughout environments

Issues
In same AZ VM restore from RSV to overwrite an existing VM
Host Encrypted and disk exclude not supported for VM restore, and does not work 
 Critical for SQL VM restore, currently leads to incompliancy for consistency target 
 In same AZ VM restore from RSV as new VM (current IaC blocker, customer issue) 
 Changes the option CreateFrom
 Blocks use of current IaC pipeline. Conflicts the consistency requirement as restored VM differ from originally provisioned VM
Cross AZ restore from RSV ZRS storage
Blocked due VM encryption not supported (host encryption) 
 RSV backup / restore with GRS 
 Not supported for Premium SSD v2 in Sweden Central. Capacity plan far in future, can it be improved? 
 VM Azure Compute Gallery Image 
 Complains about NVMe storage interface and refers to SCSi while image provisioning (May be Image properties issue) 
 Use of ASR (Azure Site Recovery) 
 Would add additional agent requirement, may lead variation between servers if not all, can not be use with SQL server 
 Preview for Premium SSD v2 
 Cost impact
original_issue.impact: The Finnish Tax Administration is migrating Gentax from on-prem to Azure. It has approximately 430 servers to migrate. And currently they are looking at the DR options in Azure. Timeframe: Customer needs this ASAP Impact: They need a way to backup their Gentax solution using a designed security baseline and features.

system_analysis.context_summary: Capacity request for SQL Database in Sweden with user requesting a feature involving servers related to sql with high business impact.
system_analysis.reasoning: The inquiry highlights multiple blockers and limitations in Azure Recovery Services Vault related to VM restore, encryption, and storage compatibility, which directly impact the customer's ability to implement their DR strategy for a critical migration project. The stated impact and urgency indicate a critical business impact. The focus on unsupported features and capacity planning aligns with the 'capacity_limits' category and 'reporting_issue' intent.

corrections.correction_notes: The user is asking for additional capabilities in Azure Recovery Services which is not a capacity issue, it is a missing feature

original_issue.title: Vodafone requires 32-bit ASN support in Azure Route Server
original_issue.description: Vodafone is working with ISD and CSU on a new Azure network topology and SDWAN integration approach based on Azure Route Server. The new topology is meant to enable Vodafone to migrate multiple on-prem datacenters (Germany, UK, Italy,...) to Azure. Vodafone has exhausted 16-bit private ASNs and is currently using 32-bit ASNs in their SDWAN. Azure Route Server does not currently support 32-bit ASNs (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/route-server/route-server-faq#can-i-use-32-bit-4-byte-asns). Vodafone requested that support for 32-bit ASNs is implemented in Azure Route Server in CY26.
original_issue.impact: Without support for 32-bit ASNs, Vodafone will not roll out the new network topology. The existing network topology (which relies on native Azure VPN Gateways for SDWAN integration) cannot scale up to the throughput required by Vodafone. If Vodafone cannot move to the new topology, some workloads will not be migrated to Azure.

context_analysis.context_summary: The customer is explicitly requesting the implementation of 32-bit ASN support in Azure Route Server, which is a missing capability they need for their new network topology and SDWAN integration. This aligns with the feature_request category and requesting_feature intent. The stated impact indicates a high business impact as it affects their migration plans and scalability.

original_issue.title: Disconnected Container - new version require more RAM
original_issue.description: Problem: Customer renewed Azure AI Translator Disconnected Tier on 24.12.2025. On 29.12.2025, they attempted to use the new licence key to extend their existing container image configuration, but the key did not work. The customer downloaded a new version of the container image, which worked with the new licence key but required almost double the amount of RAM, causing memory exhaustion during container startup in Kubernetes. As a result, the customer can now only use pl/eng instead of the previous pl,en,fr,de,es,it + ru. PG confirmed that models supporting AVX-512 and AVX2 were added, which increased the minimum RAM requirements a few months ago. This change was neither announced nor published anywhere (documentation still shows the old requirements: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/translator/containers/install-run?tabs=connected#recommended-cpu-cores-and-memory). The customer attempted to extend the licence on an unchanged environment and is escalating the lack of notification about the requirements change. Currently, PG suggests using the old key with the old container image, which should work until 20.01.2026, giving the customer time to find a way to increase RAM. This solution is not acceptable
original_issue.impact: 1. Service continuity risk -degraded translation capability - customer can only use PL/ENG translation to fit in the requirement with existing capacity, creating critical impact on the feature list used by all Pekao employees 2. Operational burden + unplanned cost (infra resizing, deployment rework, firefighting) - getting additional RAM during WW memory shortage is hard for the customer. Without a earlier warning about requirements change we put the customer against the wall 3. Trust / relationship impact (predictability of AI partnership, perception of “surprise” change) - customer is furious about this situation, expecting help.

system_analysis.context_summary: The customer is reporting a technical issue related to a container image update that caused memory exhaustion and degraded service functionality. They are escalating the lack of notification about the requirements change and the operational burden caused by this situation. The critical impact on service continuity and trust indicates an escalation request rather than a feature request.

original_issue.title: Vodafone requires 32-bit ASN support in Azure Route Server
original_issue.description: Vodafone is working with ISD and CSU on a new Azure network topology and SDWAN integration approach based on Azure Route Server. The new topology is meant to enable Vodafone to migrate multiple on-prem datacenters (Germany, UK, Italy,...) to Azure. Vodafone has exhausted 16-bit private ASNs and is currently using 32-bit ASNs in their SDWAN. Azure Route Server does not currently support 32-bit ASNs (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/route-server/route-server-faq#can-i-use-32-bit-4-byte-asns). Vodafone requested that support for 32-bit ASNs is implemented in Azure Route Server in CY26.
original_issue.impact: Without support for 32-bit ASNs, Vodafone will not roll out the new network topology. The existing network topology (which relies on native Azure VPN Gateways for SDWAN integration) cannot scale up to the throughput required by Vodafone. If Vodafone cannot move to the new topology, some workloads will not be migrated to Azure.

context_analysis.context_summary: The customer is explicitly requesting the implementation of 32-bit ASN support in Azure Route Server, which is a missing capability they need for their new network topology and SDWAN integration. This aligns with the feature_request category and requesting_feature intent. The stated impact indicates a high business impact as it affects their migration plans and scalability.

original_issue.title: Vodafone requires 32-bit ASN support in Azure Route Server
original_issue.description: Vodafone is working with ISD and CSU on a new Azure network topology and SDWAN integration approach based on Azure Route Server. The new topology is meant to enable Vodafone to migrate multiple on-prem datacenters (Germany, UK, Italy,...) to Azure. Vodafone has exhausted 16-bit private ASNs and is currently using 32-bit ASNs in their SDWAN. Azure Route Server does not currently support 32-bit ASNs (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/route-server/route-server-faq#can-i-use-32-bit-4-byte-asns). Vodafone requested that support for 32-bit ASNs is implemented in Azure Route Server in CY26.
original_issue.impact: Without support for 32-bit ASNs, Vodafone will not roll out the new network topology. The existing network topology (which relies on native Azure VPN Gateways for SDWAN integration) cannot scale up to the throughput required by Vodafone. If Vodafone cannot move to the new topology, some workloads will not be migrated to Azure.

context_analysis.context_summary: The customer is explicitly requesting the implementation of 32-bit ASN support in Azure Route Server, which is a missing capability they need for their new network topology and SDWAN integration. original_issue.title: Ability to Generate AI Meeting Summaries Without Persisting Transcripts After Meeting Ends
original_issue.description:
Telekom requires a capability within Teams/Copilot for Microsoft 365 to generate AI meeting summaries directly at the end of a meeting, based on a real-time transcript, while ensuring that the transcript is automatically deleted immediately afterward to meet strict compliance and data‑retention policies.
Currently, meeting summaries rely on transcripts that must remain stored after the meeting. Telekom's compliance framework prohibits retaining transcripts beyond the meeting duration. They need Microsoft to provide a compliant mode where ephemeral transcripts are used only for generating the summary, without ever being stored post‑meeting.
The ask:
Introduce a "no-retention transcript" mode for meetings.
Allow Copilot to consume the transcript in memory only, produce the summary, and then ensure the transcript is permanently deleted.
Ensure this feature is technically and legally compliant and clearly documented for data‑protection verification.
original_issue.impact: If Microsoft does not provide this functionality: Copilot Meeting Summaries cannot be activated for Telekom. Over 100,000+ potential Copilot for M365 users may be blocked from one of the highest‑value experiences. This could significantly delay or jeopardize Telekom’s Copilot rollout decision. Loss of trust in Microsoft as a provider that can support strict European compliance requirements. Potential reputational impact for Microsoft in other German enterprise customers with similar data‑retention rules. This feature is critical for securing Telekom’s Copilot deployment and future upsell potential.

context_analysis.context_summary: Data sovereignty and compliance inquiry for Europe involving copilot, microsoft 365, teams with high business impact.

original_issue.title: Vodafone requires 32-bit ASN support in Azure Route Server
original_issue.description: Vodafone is working with ISD and CSU on a new Azure network topology and SDWAN integration approach based on Azure Route Server. The new topology is meant to enable Vodafone to migrate multiple on-prem datacenters (Germany, UK, Italy,...) to Azure. Vodafone has exhausted 16-bit private ASNs and is currently using 32-bit ASNs in their SDWAN. Azure Route Server does not currently support 32-bit ASNs (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/route-server/route-server-faq#can-i-use-32-bit-4-byte-asns). Vodafone requested that support for 32-bit ASNs is implemented in Azure Route Server in CY26.
original_issue.impact: Without support for 32-bit ASNs, Vodafone will not roll out the new network topology. The existing network topology (which relies on native Azure VPN Gateways for SDWAN integration) cannot scale up to the throughput required by Vodafone. If Vodafone cannot move to the new topology, some workloads will not be migrated to Azure.
